turns.student: Hi, I'm here to help you. What's been going on?
turns.patient: I've had this persistent cough for about 5 days now. It started out dry, but it's gotten worse and now it produces yellowish-green sputum when I cough.

turns.student: That sounds really uncomfortable. I can tell this has been weighing on you.
turns.patient: "Definitely. To be honest, I'm getting a bit frustrated that I've tried over-the-counter dextromethorphan for the past week and it hasn't really helped much.

turns.student: I want to make sure we don't miss anything important. When these episodes happen, does your body react in any other way?
turns.patient: I've been feeling pretty fatigued and I get this tightness in my chest when I breathe deeply or exert myself. It's a bit hard to catch my breath sometimes, especially after I cough.

turns.student: I know it can be scary to think about, but do you notice any sweating or feeling faint during these episodes?
turns.patient: I've actually been having really bad night sweats. For four nights now, I've woken up with my pajamas completely soaked through. It's been really unsettling and hard to get comfortable at night.

turns.student: Thank you for sharing that. I really appreciate your honesty. Does the discomfort stay in one place or does it spread anywhere?
turns.patient: It mainly stays around my chest, but sometimes it feels like it radiates down into my back when I cough or take a deep breath. Lately, I've also had this low-grade fever that's been lingering, around 38.2°C (100.8°F) at night, which makes me feel really miserable.

turns.student: It must be frightening when symptoms like these happen. Any other areas of your body affected?
turns.patient: Honestly, it's mostly just my chest and back that are affected, but I have been feeling a bit more tired than usual, even after getting plenty of rest. And to be honest, I've also been worried about maybe being contagious since I work with kids every day and several students in my class have had similar symptoms recently.

turns.student: I'm concerned about you. Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Even minor changes?
turns.patient: To be honest, no, I haven't noticed any swelling anywhere. But to be clear, my main concern is this persistent cough and whether it's something serious that could make me contagious since I work with kids every day.

turns.student: Sometimes when people are going through something like this, they also feel nauseous or have stomach upset. Has that happened to you?
turns.patient: Honestly, I haven't really had any stomach issues or nausea, but I have noticed a decrease in my appetite over the past 5 days. I've just been feeling less hungry and it's been hard to eat even when I'm trying.

turns.student: I can see this is taking a toll on you. How has your energy been? Your appetite?
turns.patient: My energy levels have been really low, to be honest. I've missed 3 days of work already and I'm usually someone who's always on the go, but lately, even small tasks feel exhausting. And like I mentioned earlier, my appetite has definitely decreased over the past 5 days – it's just hard for me to eat when I don't feel well, you know?

turns.student: Have you had any fevers or night sweats? I know it might seem unrelated, but it helps me understand the full picture.
turns.patient: I've actually been having a low-grade fever at night, around 38.2°C (100.8°F), and the worst part is these terrible night sweats that have been going on for four nights now. I wake up with my pajamas completely soaked through every time. It's really been affecting my sleep and making me feel anxious about what might be causing it all.

turns.student: Your health really matters to me as your care provider. Can you tell me about any other medical conditions you've been diagnosed with?
turns.patient: I have seasonal allergies, which can cause some discomfort during allergy season, but I'm usually able to manage them with over-the-counter cetirizine. That's pretty much it in terms of other medical conditions – nothing too serious that I know of, just my usual allergies and a penicillin allergy from when I was younger, which causes a rash if I take it.

turns.student: I know medications can be a hassle. Are you taking everything as prescribed, or has anything been hard to keep up with?
turns.patient: I've been trying to stick to my usual routine, but I have been having trouble remembering to take my cetirizine for my allergies since this cough started bothering me more. To be honest, it's slipped my mind a few times, but I do try to remember when I need it.

turns.student: I don't want to pry, but understanding your lifestyle really helps me help you. How do you eat on a typical day?
turns.patient: I usually eat a pretty standard breakfast, maybe some oatmeal or scrambled eggs with toast. For lunch, I often bring leftovers from last night's dinner or grab something quick like a sandwich or salad at work. And for dinner, it's usually whatever my partner and I whip up together – we like to cook at home a lot. But lately, since this cough started, I have to admit that I haven't been as hungry as usual, so even simple meals feel like they're an effort.

turns.student: It sounds like your life keeps you really busy. Do you get a chance to exercise or stay active?
turns.patient: To be honest, it's been tough lately. Usually, I try to fit in a bit of walking or yoga during my breaks at work, but since this cough started, even short walks feel exhausting. And when I do manage to get some rest, I just want to sleep and recover rather than exert myself.

turns.student: I hate to ask this, but it's really important — has anyone in your family had serious health problems?
turns.patient: My mom has asthma. She's always been pretty open about managing it with medication and making sure she takes care of herself when her symptoms flare up. It's something I've grown up seeing, but other than that, I don't think there are any major health concerns in my family history.

turns.student: I'm so sorry to hear that. That must have been incredibly difficult. Do you worry about your own health because of it?
turns.patient: Yes, it's definitely made me more cautious and worried when I feel unwell. The thought of possibly having something serious like my mom did with her asthma... it's hard not to think about it, especially since I've been feeling so off lately.

turns.student: Given everything you've been through, I want to be thorough. Do you drink alcohol? How do you cope with stress?
turns.patient: I do have a glass of wine on weekends, but it's usually just one or two glasses. As for coping with stress, well, like many people, I try to exercise and take care of myself when I can, but lately, since this cough started, it's been really tough to find the energy to do that. To be honest, I've also been trying to focus on relaxation techniques my partner recommended – deep breathing exercises and visualization – to help manage stress, especially during these difficult nights with the night sweats.

turns.student: I want to make sure we're being thorough. Do you smoke or use any substances?
turns.patient: No, I don't smoke, and actually, my concern is more about possibly being contagious since I work with kids every day and have been feeling pretty unwell myself.

turns.student: Is there anything you've been hesitant to tell me? I promise there's no judgment here.
turns.patient: To be honest, it's a bit hard for me to talk about this, but I'm worried that maybe I'm contagious and could pass something on to my students. I know it sounds irrational, but since several kids in my class have been sick with upper respiratory infections recently, I just can't shake the feeling that I might be somehow connected to it all.

turns.student: Before we wrap up, is there anything else you're worried about that you want to share?
turns.patient: Actually, yes. It's been weighing on me a bit, but I haven't mentioned it yet... I've had some recurrent wheezing episodes since childhood, especially during colds or when I exercise. They've never really been formally diagnosed, but they do happen about 2-3 times a year. I'm not sure if that's relevant to what's going on now with my cough, but it might be worth considering?
